result.reasoning: Verified against current code. tracing.py:30 reads `TRACING_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006"))` at module top level. Reproduced: `ASSAY_TRACING_PORT=notanint .venv/bin/python -c 'import assay_engine.observability.tracing'` raises `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notanint'` at line 30, with no mention of the offending variable. The module is imported at top of both orchestration/graph.py:23 and pipeline.py:62, so a single misconfigured env var aborts the orchestration/pipeline import path before any code runs. The analogous case was deliberately fixed via the `_int_env` helper in persistence/vectorstore.py (lines 20-21, 33) documented as #H-022 ("A bare int(os.environ[...]) at import time raises an opaque ValueError that doesn't say WHICH var was misconfigured, and it fires during import"). This is the one remaining bare-int import-time parse, making it a real inconsistency/regression against an established invariant. The proposed fix (reuse _int_env or inline a try/except naming the var) is correct and low-effort. Severity is low: default works, trigger is operator misconfiguration only, but blast radius (whole pipeline import fails with an un-attributed error) is real for an operator-deployed tool.

Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. tracing.py:30 reads `TRACING_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006"))` at module top level. Reproduced exactly: `ASSAY_TRACING_PORT=notanint .venv/bin/python -c 'import assay_engine.observability.tracing'` raises an uncaught `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notanint'` at line 30 during import; the message never names ASSAY_TRACING_PORT. This is the exact failure mode that the `_int_env` helper in persistence/vectorstore.py:20-30 was deliberately created to fix for ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT (documented as #H-022: opaque ValueError at import that doesn't say which var). So this is a genuine inconsistency/regression against an established hardening invariant, on the orchestration/pipeline import path. Fix is trivial (reuse _int_env). Minor correction to the finding's claim: tracing.py is NOT the only remaining bare-int import-time parse — checkpoint.py:61 (_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS) also uses a bare int(os.environ...) at module top level. That doesn't undermine the finding for tracing.py but the 'one remaining' phrasing is inaccurate. Severity low is appropriate: default works, trigger is operator misconfiguration, no security/data impact; the blast radius (whole pipeline import aborts) and un-attributed error are real but minor production-readiness concerns.

Verified against the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py.

Mechanism confirmed:
- Line 336: `stability_threshold: float = 0.9` — concrete non-None default. By contrast, alpha (line 334) has no default and predicted_direction (line 337) uses a `None` sentinel.
- Lines 363-368: the supplied value is always cross-checked via `_resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.stability_threshold, stability_threshold, "stability_threshold")`.
- Lines 278-285: `_resolve_locked_param` raises ValueError when `locked is not None and supplied is not None and supplied != locked`.

Because an omitted argument arrives as the literal 0.9 (never None), there is no way to distinguish "caller omitted it" from "caller passed 0.9". So for any hypothesis that pre-registers a stability_threshold != 0.9, calling confirm_whole_corpus WITHOUT restating it sends supplied=0.9, locked=e.g. 0.5, and the check raises "stability_threshold argument 0.9 contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered stability_threshold 0.5" — exactly the spurious error described.

Reproducibility confirmed:
- Hypothesis model (hypothesis.py:80) declares `stability_threshold: float | None = None` and validates any value in (0,1] (lines 95-97), so locking 0.5 is legal.
- The documented contract of _resolve_locked_param (lines 273-276) explicitly says "The locked value wins. If nothing was locked, the supplied value stands" — the intent is that omission should defer to the lock, which the current signature makes impossible.
- The regression test (tests/test_confirm.py:274-287) locks stability_threshold to exactly 0.9 (line 278), which equals the default, so the false-positive omission path is never exercised — masking the bug.

This is a real correctness defect: it directly defeats the HARKing-prevention feature it was meant to support. A study pre-registering a non-default stability bar cannot rely on "the locked value wins"; it is forced to re-supply the exact number, and any drift silently turns a legitimate confirmation into a hard error. The proposed fix (None sentinel, resolve, then apply 0.9 default) is correct and mirrors the existing predicted_direction handling.

Severity: medium. It is a genuine, reproducible bug worth fixing, but it raises a hard error (fail-loud, no silent wrong verdict) and has a trivial workaround (restate the locked threshold), so it is not high/critical.

Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py. The suffix gate is exactly as described: _copy_scrubbed (lines 100-118) only calls _scrub when srcf.suffix == ".jsonl" (line 104); all other files fall to the else branch with data = raw (line 107), copied byte-for-byte. The capture ingests all file types via subtree.rglob("*") (line 138), so .json/.meta.json/.txt/.js all reach the raw branch. _scrub (lines 79-91) holds the #F-024/#G-007 path+email+username redaction logic, which therefore never runs on non-jsonl files. PoC reproduced exactly: 2487 (json) + 36 (js) + 51776 (txt) unredacted /Users/[REDACTED] occurrences in non-jsonl files, 0 in .jsonl — proving the patterns work and only the gate skips them. Operator PII confirmed leaked: 82 [REDACTED] and 10 [REDACTED] occurrences. Publication path confirmed: .githooks/pre-commit lines 10-11 run the script then git add transcripts/; 1251 non-jsonl files are already tracked. This is a real, reproducible defect that turns a stated/hardened redaction control into a silent no-op for the dominant file types and has already committed operator PII. The recommended fix (scrub by content with try-decode/UnicodeDecodeError fallback to raw) is correct. Rated high rather than critical only because the leaked data is the operator's own identity (home path, hostname email, personal email) rather than third-party secrets/credentials, but it is genuine PII in the tracked tree intended to be masked.

Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py. Line 336 declares `stability_threshold: float = 0.9` (concrete default), while the companion alpha at line 334 has no default. Lines 363-368 cross-check the supplied value against the pre-registered one via _resolve_locked_param, whose contract (lines 273-276) is 'the locked value wins; if nothing was locked, the supplied value stands' — which requires distinguishing an omitted argument from a passed one. With a concrete 0.9 default, omission is indistinguishable from supplying 0.9, so for any hypothesis locking stability_threshold != 0.9, the omitted call hits `supplied is not None and supplied != locked` (line 279) and raises. The Hypothesis model supports locking stability_threshold to arbitrary values in (0,1] (hypothesis.py:80,95). Reproduced the PoC verbatim with the venv interpreter: locking stability_threshold=0.5 and omitting it at confirm time yields `ValueError: stability_threshold argument 0.9 contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered stability_threshold 0.5` — the caller never passed 0.9. The existing regression test (tests/test_confirm.py:274-287) masks the bug by locking to exactly 0.9 (== the default), so the false-positive path is never exercised. This defeats the #H-001 HARKing-prevention feature it was meant to enable, with a hard error and a misleading message. The proposed fix (None sentinel mirroring predicted_direction, apply 0.9 after resolution) is correct.

Confirmed in current code. scripts/capture_transcripts.py:104-107 gates _scrub on srcf.suffix == ".jsonl"; the else branch copies raw bytes verbatim. The main() capture loop (lines 131-143) rglob("*")s the entire session subtree and routes every file type through _copy_scrubbed, so .json/.meta.json/.txt/.js files bypass redaction entirely. Reproduced the PoC exactly: non-.jsonl /Users/[REDACTED] occurrences json=2487, js=36, txt=51776; .jsonl=0 (proving patterns work and the suffix gate is the cause). Also reproduced 82 occurrences of [REDACTED] and 10 of [REDACTED]. git ls-files confirms the leaky non-.jsonl files (e.g. tool-results/*.txt, subagents/*.meta.json) are tracked/committed. The bypassed control is real: _SECRET_PATTERNS (lines 38-55) and _USERNAME_RE (line 76) implement the #F-024/#G-007/#H-018 email/home-path/username masking, which is silently no-op'd for the dominant file types. The suggested fix (try decode+scrub, fall back to raw on UnicodeDecodeError) is correct since capture only ingests text. Real, reproducible, and worth fixing.
